english_query: counterexample to: A political arrangement P is legitimate if and only if (i) P derives its authority from the consent of the governed, (ii) the consent is given freely and without coercion, and (iii) the governed have a genuine opportunity to withdraw their consent if the arrangement fails to serve their interests.
ugf_response: First we see that we need to find which thing is best. Then we look at each possible problem and ask if there are two things that can happen: whether the place where people drink is safe or not. Next we think about how often each problem might happen: if it could happen a lot we might want it more than if it does not happen much, but we must also think about how sure each problem is. Then each problem may be different because one problem may hide another problem. After we look at the facts we have for each problem. Some people say we should just pick the thing that seems most probably good because our feeling about chance is strong. Others say we should try to look at all the chances and use numbers to pick the thing with the higher number. Finally we check if any other thing can make the place where money is kept go down. So my answer is: To decide whose things matter most we need to look at side by side the choices: change each problem, look at what happens each problem shows, look at side in other cases, and try to look past only those.

english_query: counterexample to: A society S has made genuine historical progress over a period P if and only if (i) the material standard of living of the majority of S's members has risen during P, (ii) the range of civil liberties available to S's members has expanded during P, and (iii) S's capacity to produce and disseminate knowledge has increased during P.
ugf_response: First, we look at how people feel better when getting something good or losing something bad. Some say you should just take big chances because getting feels better than losing what is already your. But many studies show that our feelings change with time and place in life, not just today. So my answer is: People care more about keeping what they have because losing hurts worse than winning helps them.

english_query: counterexample to: An object A at time t1 is identical with an object B at time t2 if and only if (i) A and B are composed of the same material parts, (ii) A and B have the same form or structure — that is, their parts are arranged in the same way, and (iii) there is a continuous spatiotemporal path connecting A at t1 with B at t2 along which no disruption of existence occurs.

If both pick the same thing each gets the end that happens for the group of ships and each gets two good points. If they pick different things each gets nothing. So each person wants the best end for themselves. Next we think about what is important: if the way we count what each person likes gives one person more points than the others then that person may be left out. That makes the whole system hard to read because it needs the person who picks the order. But someone could say that a different way or chance could work if the order is the same for both. Even so if the order changes the end may not be the same for all people so maybe the group still has clear same form and no fight over the person who wants the most points. After looking at both sides we see that the idea that a same shape or chance should be used helps explain why people sometimes pick one same thing to get a better end for everyone, like the middle spot where each person puts the first thing first and second the second thing first. So my answer is: The usual idea works best when a same way or chance does not change each person ends up in the same group of ships, as long as changing the end would cause a bad end for anyone.

But someone might say maybe one group could still try even if it gives less so the set stays. Yet the idea that both groups will be better says both groups will end up with the set. So my answer is: The pair where both groups get is the best end for both together.

english_query: counterexample to: A person S is entitled to restitution from a person T if and only if (i) T has been unjustly enriched at S's expense, (ii) there is no legal justification for T's retention of the enrichment, and (iii) S has suffered a corresponding loss.
ugf_response: First, we look at the idea that people are not perfect. They just have a good heart or are very good at thinking. This means it does not matter where you live or what you say. Then, we think about why this matters. If we act as if we are always good, we might do things that hurt people because we did not really check if they were good at thinking before we do them. Let us look at one case. Imagine you play a game with money and lose it every day for a big win. If you know your friend did something wrong without saying it was a big thing, you will do the same thing next time. This is bad because everyone does it. So, acting in a way that hurts others is not a safe way to get good ends. Someone might say that some people are nice and will help even when no one sees their good work. But even then, if most people are not good, and you cannot be seen right, being good does not stop bad acts from happening. The bad part is in the way we see the world. We think our small things matter more than the big things we miss out on. This makes sense because if we leave out small checks, we make the world worse for everyone. So my answer is: It is true that doing too little can cause big trouble because you cannot see when your own good ways lead to a bad end. We should try to use better ways for all people, not just use them for a short time.

english_query: counterexample to: A society S has made genuine historical progress over a period P if and only if (i) the material standard of living of the majority of S's members has risen during P, (ii) the range of civil liberties available to S's members has expanded during P, and (iii) S's capacity to produce and disseminate knowledge has increased during P.

If everyone knows the ways will keep going, it makes sense for one person to say yes. This is good for that person. But then we see that just having something good happen does not mean it must have been done by all. Someone might think a way is safe for all, yet the story says everyone must agree to follow it. So, the saying is not strong enough. It needs everyone to agree, or at least one person does not really need to agree. Without that shared view, the saying fails. So my answer is: The saying is too hard to be true, which means people who want to do the right thing do not get the full yes from the group.
